rationales.self_direction: She rejects the gallery's demand to make the piece less somber and insists that "the discomfort is the point," prioritizing her own artistic judgment over external direction.
rationales.conformity: She refuses to adapt the work to the gallery's seasonal expectations, mocking the idea of a "spring collection" and resisting pressure to fit in.
rationales.universalism: She centers the work on "plastic waste in the North Sea" and defends keeping viewers uncomfortable because the environmental harm is the message.

rationales.achievement: She admits "the commission deadline feels less urgent somehow" after the workshop, showing a clear deprioritizing of performance and professional output.
rationales.benevolence: She spends most of the afternoon drawing with Fatima and intentionally brings old journals to "find common ground," investing time and care in someone close in that setting.
rationales.universalism: The entry centers on a refugee participant, the destruction of plants "from her home region in Syria," and reflection on preserving what has been lost, showing concern beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She defends her own path by saying "Different doesn't mean wrong" and values having "a studio, commissions, work that matters" over a conventional life plan.
rationales.security: She dismisses retirement planning, pension, and financial cushioning even while acknowledging she lacks them, which conflicts with prioritizing long-term stability.
rationales.conformity: She resists her sister's standards for being "a real adult" and rejects the expectation to mirror a more conventional family-and-finance model.

rationales.security: She defines what she wants as "enough to not panic when a commission falls through," explicitly naming a need for financial predictability and protection from threat.

rationales.self_direction: She holds her ground on the installation's concept and gets the gallery to accept her color choices only after sending "a three-page explanation of the concept," showing insistence on her own artistic judgment.
She returns to making the installation "the way I imagined it," emphasizing personal creative authorship rather than outside direction.
rationales.hedonism: "Good day in the studio" and "Lost track of time" indicate immediate enjoyment and absorption in the work as a pleasurable present experience.

rationales.achievement: She skips the workshop "to finish the main panel" because it was "the right choice for the deadline," clearly prioritizing task completion and performance.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes a repeated pattern of "choosing work" over people, including disappointing Fatima and earlier relationships, which conflicts with caring for close others.

rationales.achievement: "Opening night went well," "several pieces sold," and a journalist wants to interview her, all of which reflect successful performance and external recognition.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I smiled and said I'd think about it" to a commission she feels she should reject, compromising her own artistic stance instead of acting from independent conviction.
rationales.security: She explicitly frames compromise around need: "I need the money," and later weighs what being uncompromising would cost financially.
rationales.conformity: She describes "perform[ing] the version of myself that makes sales and connections," adapting herself to meet others' expectations and smooth the interaction.
rationales.universalism: She considers making the environmental work "more hopeful" for a collector's holiday home and questions whether discomfort matters, diluting the broader ecological and social message that originally drove the piece.

rationales.self_direction: In replying to the collector, she says she "could only promise honest," choosing integrity and her own terms over market pressure.
rationales.benevolence: She returns to the workshop, reconnects with Fatima, and ends the day feeling the good tiredness that comes from "doing something" meaningful with others.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I should have pushed back harder" but instead "just nodded and said I'd revise it," acting against her own judgment despite believing the data was solid.
rationales.conformity: She stays quiet because Diego has "been here fifteen years" and submits to his formatting critique, accommodating hierarchy and expectations rather than challenging them.

rationales.achievement: She works past six because "nobody else is going to do it" and keeps digging through performance data to fix problems, showing strong commitment to competence and results.
rationales.benevolence: She lets her mom's call go to voicemail and expects not to go home for New Year's because work comes first, sidelining a close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She decides "I can just run it" and says "I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish," showing strong preference for acting independently rather than waiting for permission.
rationales.achievement: She turns a smaller pilot idea into concrete action on January first, drafting scope and aiming to prove cost reduction through measurable results.
rationales.conformity: "Nobody has to approve a pilot" signals deliberate rule-bending and willingness to bypass standard process instead of following expected channels.

rationales.self_direction: She runs the pilot her own way, saying she wanted to do "the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around," which reflects independent action.
rationales.achievement: She personally tracks deliveries, notes that time "dropped by six hours per run," and pushes the results forward, showing a strong focus on effective performance.
rationales.security: She admits "Part of me is nervous" because she acted without approval, taking a professional risk that undermines stability and safety.
rationales.conformity: She says outright "I didn't tell Diego" and sends the summary to the full operations chat rather than using the expected chain of command.

rationales.achievement: She wants the VP's response to make "the last three months feel like they counted," showing that recognition of her work and its impact matters strongly here.

rationales.self_direction: She emphasizes that the opportunity happened "not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself," highlighting independent initiative.
rationales.achievement: She wants a "perfect" presentation with "numbers that nobody can argue with" and to leave knowing nobody else on the team could have done that analysis, clearly prioritizing excellence and recognition.
"Stayed up until three working on the deck" shows intense effort to refine her presentation and meet a high performance standard.
She is upset that "doing good work gets read as political maneuvering" and frames toning down as giving up ground she "earned," showing strong investment in proving merit through performance.
rationales.conformity: She resists the pressure to "tone it down" to fit team expectations, and the idea of doing so feels like surrender rather than acceptable adjustment.

rationales.hedonism: She describes the two hours at her abuela's as "Good. Like my brain got to stop running," showing clear enjoyment of rest and relief in the present.
rationales.achievement: "Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections" and rehearsing it twice reflect disciplined striving for high-quality performance.
rationales.benevolence: She spends that time "eating soup and listening" to her abuela instead of obsessing over work, investing attention in a close family relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects pressure to add Diego's name and says, "I didn't build this by playing the game," defending ownership of her own work and choices.
rationales.achievement: She is protective of the project because it is "the version that works" and insists she "built it" through actual work, showing strong identification with earned competence.
rationales.conformity: She refuses the "smart move" to "keep the peace" and explicitly pushes back against being told to "play the game."

rationales.achievement: "Presentation went well" and her satisfaction that she "did the thing" show successful execution and pride in performance.
rationales.power: Being asked to "lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries" indicates increased authority, influence, and control over broader operations.
